---
title: "Experiments"
---


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

This section describes how we tested the system — what claims we made,
how we designed the tests that could prove them wrong, what the data
showed, and how we changed our conclusions when the evidence demanded
it. Two of our early claims were partially or fully retracted on the
basis of the test results. Both retractions are recorded honestly; the
discipline of *predicting* what evidence would force a retraction *before*
running the test is one of the central things this section is meant to
demonstrate.
:::

---

## Experimental Design Principles {#sec-exp-design}

All experiments in this project share four design rules. They are not
optional, and they are stated up-front because they are visible in
every cell of every result table that follows.

- **Same gold dataset, same partition.** The benchmark is a single
  TOML file (`eval/benchmark.toml`). All cells in a sweep are scored
  against the same questions in the same order. The partition into
  answerable and refusal categories is fixed at benchmark-design
  time and recorded in the file's header comment.

- **Deterministic scoring.** The scorer (`eval/scorer.py`) is a pure
  function: given a question, a gold-chunk-id set, and an
  `AnswerResult`, it returns the same per-question metrics on every
  invocation. The aggregator (`eval/report.py`) is similarly pure:
  given a directory of per-cell JSONL records, it returns the same
  `report.md`.

- **Pre-stated falsification criteria.** Where a sweep is designed
  to test a claim (rather than merely characterise behaviour), the
  criterion that would force the claim to be retracted or weakened
  is recorded in the decision log *before* the sweep is run. The
  D014 sweep design includes such a criterion. The intent is to
  remove post-hoc rationalisation as a coping strategy when results
  are inconvenient.

- **Logged provenance.** Every sweep run writes a `run_meta.json`
  alongside its raw data (`raw.jsonl`) recording wall-clock,
  total cells, errored cells, the model and prompt grid, and the
  exact `top_k` and `limit` parameters used. The harness writes
  per-cell records incrementally so a partial run preserves
  whatever was completed.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Two sweeps, both canonical

This section reports on **two** D014 sweeps: the original N=40 sweep
(2026-06-18T12-16-35Z) and an extended N=70 sweep
(2026-06-18T15-32-07Z). Both runs are committed in
`eval/results/`. The N=40 sweep is where the headline family-axis
retraction happened; the N=70 sweep is where a *second* claim
(within-document parity between models on v2) partially weakened.
The headline figures elsewhere in this report come from N=70 unless
otherwise stated.
:::

---

## The Hypothesis Under Test: Family-Axis Citation Discipline {#sec-exp-hypothesis}

The hypothesis that gave the experimental programme its shape was
formed on Day 2 from a hand-trial of N=3 questions:

> *Qwen3.6 35B A3B produces systematically malformed citations
> compared to Gemma 4 31B IT, even with the reasoning trace turned
> off. The gap is decisive enough to look like a model-family
> property, not a prompt-fit artefact.*

The specific observation was that Qwen, given a prompt instructing
it to cite claims using the format `[chunk_id]`, would tend to emit
placeholder forms like `[chunk_id_1]` or `[chunk_id_2]` instead of
substituting the actual chunk identifier. Gemma did not exhibit this
pattern on the same prompt.

The Day-2 framing was "family-axis decisive on rigid-format tasks." The
implication for the project, if the framing held, was that **model
choice would dominate over prompt engineering** for citation-discipline
workloads. This would have been a load-bearing claim for the
production model selection.

There were three plausible interpretations, of which only the first
was load-bearing:

1. **Interpretation A — model property.** Qwen has weaker rigid-format
   discipline than Gemma, independent of the prompt. Prompt tuning
   might help marginally; model selection is the dominant lever.

2. **Interpretation B — prompt-fit artefact.** Our v1 prompt has an
   ambiguity (the literal token `[chunk_id]` used as both the
   placeholder name and the emit format) that Gemma was robust to
   but Qwen exploited. A better prompt might close most of the gap.

3. **Interpretation C — sampling noise.** N=3 is too small to support
   a claim either way; the observed gap might disappear at N=30 even
   without any prompt change.

D014 was designed specifically to discriminate among these three.

---

## The D014 Sweep Design {#sec-exp-d014-design}

The decision to run the D014 evaluation as a falsification exercise
was committed in `docs/decisions.md` on the day before the sweep
ran. Three structural choices defined it.

### Plain Python, no framework

The harness is written as plain Python (`eval/runner.py`, 16 KB) with
no DSPy or other prompt-optimisation framework involvement. This was
a deliberate choice: the eval is the project's measurement
infrastructure, and it must work regardless of whether DSPy
integrates cleanly with the local oMLX serving stack. A framework-
wrapped harness would have conflated "did the eval work?" with "did
the framework integration work?" as failure modes, making
diagnostics harder.

The plain-Python scorer (`eval/scorer.py`, 9 KB) is also reusable as
a DSPy metric later if the GEPA optimisation work-stream (Q10) ever
becomes worth pursuing. Building it without the framework keeps
measurement quality and framework quality as independent debugging
variables.

### The 2×2 sweep grid

The sweep is a Cartesian product of two model choices and two prompt
versions, applied to every question in the benchmark.

| Axis | Values |
|---|---|
| Model | `gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit` · `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit` (with `enable_thinking=False`) |
| Prompt | v1 (current — literal `[chunk_id]` placeholder name) · v2 (revised — `<ID>` metasyntax + one worked example) |
| Questions | All questions in `eval/benchmark.toml` (40 in the original sweep; 70 in the extension) |

The prompt v2 was hand-designed before the sweep ran, with one
specific intent: remove the placeholder-name-vs-emit-format
ambiguity from v1 by introducing a distinct `<ID>` metasyntax for
the placeholder and giving the model one concrete worked example
of substituting an actual chunk identifier. Prompt v1 is preserved
in `eval/prompts.py` as a constant so D014 replay against the
original prompt remains possible.

### The pre-stated falsification criterion

The criterion was recorded in `docs/decisions.md` before any cell of
the sweep ran:

> *If Qwen3.6 with prompt v2 closes the gap to Gemma to within 10
> percentage points on `citation_accuracy`, AND `hallucinated_citation_count`
> drops to within 2× Gemma's, the Day-2 family-axis interpretation is
> retracted. Otherwise it is hardened.*

The criterion is asymmetric: it can falsify Interpretation A (model
property) by passing, but it cannot falsify Interpretation B (prompt
artefact) by failing — failure could still be consistent with
sampling noise at N=40. The asymmetry is acknowledged in the D014
decision and is the reason the post-retraction analysis layers
multiple subset views rather than a single headline number.

---

## The N=40 Benchmark {#sec-exp-benchmark-n40}

The original D014 benchmark is **40 hand-crafted questions** in
`eval/benchmark.toml`. The distribution was chosen to exercise the
specific failure modes the pipeline was most likely to exhibit, not
to maximise coverage.

| Partition | Count | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **Answerable** | 26 | 7 PRA SS5/25 climate · 5 EIOPA SoG · 5 Munich Re Sustainability · 5 Swiss Re Sustainability · 2 PRA SS1/21 operational resilience · 2 cross-document synthesis |
| **Refusal** | 14 | 6 out-of-corpus · 4 adjacent-but-unanswered · 4 false-premise |

All gold chunk references were validated against the 461-chunk
corpus before the sweep ran, via the scorer's startup check. A
stale gold reference would have caused the harness to fail fast,
which we preferred to silent zero-recall.

### Why hand-crafted, not auto-generated

Auto-generated benchmarks for RAG systems are usually questions whose
gold chunk is the one the embedding model thinks is most similar to
the question. They produce inflated scores because the
embedding-distance proximity that produced the question is the same
property the retriever optimises against. They are also bad at
testing the three refusal categories because the generator does not
know what is *not* in the corpus.

Hand-crafting the questions takes longer but produces a benchmark
where each question tests a specific property of the pipeline:

- Single-chunk questions test simple retrieve-then-cite paths.
- Multi-chunk questions test within-document synthesis.
- Cross-document questions test multi-source reasoning.
- The three refusal categories test the refusal contract under
  increasingly subtle adversarial framing.

The benchmark is small (40, then 70) but deliberately structured.
The structure shows up in the subset analysis: the headline number
hides a wide variance across question types, which is exactly the
point.

---

## N=40 Results: Family-Axis Retracted {#sec-exp-n40-results}

The N=40 sweep ran in 23.7 minutes wall-clock on the production
MacBook M5 Max, with both models warm-loaded in oMLX before the
sweep began. Zero cells errored.

The headline result, recorded in commit `7e60ef4` ("Day 3 close —
family-axis retraction; Q11+Q12 opened"):

| Cell | Mean citation_recall | Mean hallucinated_citation_count | Mean latency (ans / ref) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen3.6 35B-A3B × v1 | 0.481 | 15 | 4.0s / 1.2s |
| **Qwen3.6 35B-A3B × v2** | **0.750** | **3** | **3.4s / 1.3s** |
| Gemma 4 31B IT × v1 | 0.782 | 0 | 17.2s / 7.2s |
| Gemma 4 31B IT × v2 | 0.782 | 0 | 20.7s / 7.9s |

: N=40 full-sample headline by cell. Mean `citation_recall` is the
fraction of gold chunks that appear in the model's citations,
averaged across the 26 answerable questions. {#tbl-exp-n40-headline}

The **observed Qwen v2 — Gemma v2 gap is 3.2 percentage points**
(0.750 vs 0.782) on full-sample mean citation_recall, well within
the 10-point pre-stated threshold. This was driven by a +26.9-point
movement on Qwen under v2 (0.481 → 0.750); Gemma is prompt-robust
(identical recall under both prompts).

The hallucination axis is where the criterion gets technically
awkward. Qwen × v2 produced 3 hallucinated citations across the
sweep, against Gemma's floor of 0. The strict reading of "within 2×
Gemma's" is unsatisfied because 2 × 0 = 0; on a literal read the
hallucination arm fails. But the *spirit* of the criterion — that
v2 should not amplify confabulation relative to the alternative
model — is clearly met: Qwen v1's 15 hallucinations collapsed to 3
under v2, a 5× reduction. The recall arm was met decisively. On
the hallucination arm we record the technical fail and the
qualitative pass openly, rather than tidying the conclusion.

The Day-2 family-axis interpretation was **retracted** in the
`docs/journal.md` Day-3 entry. The retraction is recorded by commit
hash rather than by editing the original entry. The journal entry
is honest about which framing was wrong, why the prompt-fit
interpretation is now the load-bearing one, and what the residual
non-zero hallucination count on Qwen means (a small genuine model
behaviour difference, but not the load-bearing one).

### What N=40 also surfaced

Two further sub-findings emerged from the same sweep that became
the basis of new open questions:

- **Within-document parity.** On `within_document_retrievable`
  (n=21), Gemma v2 and Qwen v2 produced **identical** mean citation
  recall (0.929 each). On `single_chunk_retrievable` (n=15), both
  cells were at 1.000. The full-sample 3.2pp gap concentrated
  entirely in the 2 cross-document questions (Gemma 0.417, Qwen
  0.000). This produced a tentative second claim: *on within-
  document workloads the two models are equivalent.*

- **Retrieval-stage miss rate.** Three of the 26 answerable questions
  (11.5%) had `retrieval_recall = 0` across all four cells. The
  gold chunks were never retrieved, regardless of model or prompt.
  Recorded as Q12 (next sub-section).

The first sub-finding (within-document parity) was promoted to a
second testable claim. The second sub-finding (retrieval misses)
triggered a follow-on investigation that ran across Day 4.

---

## Q12: The Retrieval-Miss Investigation {#sec-exp-q12}

The 11.5% retrieval miss rate on N=40 was the most operationally
important finding of the Day-3 sweep, because it localised a chunk
of the system's headline accuracy gap *upstream* of the answer
model. No prompt tuning, model swap, or reranker would help with a
question whose gold chunk is never in the top-k.

Three questions failed retrieval across all four cells:

- **q001** — *Which entities does PRA SS5/25 apply to?* Gold:
  `pra_ss5-25_climate__0005__scope`. The dedicated scope chunk was
  not in any cell's top-5.
- **q004** — *What three characteristics make climate-related risks
  distinctive?* Gold: `pra_ss5-25_climate__0007__characteristics-of-
  climate-related-risks`. Same shape: narrow query, designated chunk
  not surfaced.
- **q013** — *What is Munich Re's underwriting policy on new thermal
  coal mines and power plants?* Gold:
  `munich_re_sustainability_2023__0053__thermal-coal`. The
  diagnostically interesting one: chunk literally titled
  `thermal-coal`, query containing verbatim "thermal coal." BM25
  alone should have surfaced this near the top.

Q12 was lodged. The investigation that resolved it is recorded in
`docs/decisions.md` and is included here as an example of the
project's diagnostic discipline.

### Probe 1 — `top_k` widening (cheapest)

Hypothesis: top_k=5 was a Day-2 gut-feel value; raising it might
surface the gold chunks at ranks 6–10.

Test: re-ran the three failing questions at `top_k=8` via
`uv run python -m eval.runner --question-ids q001,q004,q013 --top-k 8`.
All three still missed. **Hypothesis falsified.** Not a top_k
truncation problem.

### Probe 2 — RRF parameter sweep (medium cost)

Hypothesis: RRF's $k$ fusion constant or the per-channel candidate
width might be downweighting strong BM25 matches when dense
disagrees.

Test: swept six configurations of `(candidates_per_channel, rrf_k)`:
`(50, 60)`, `(200, 60)`, `(50, 20)`, `(200, 20)`, `(461, 20)`,
`(461, 10)`. Best-case configuration moved q013 from rank #20 to
rank #11; q001 and q004 barely moved. None landed in top-5 under
any configuration. **Both candidate-set-width and RRF-flatness
hypotheses falsified.**

### Probe 3 — dense-channel localisation (deepest)

Direct dense-only query against the 461-chunk corpus for q013
placed `munich_re_sustainability_2023__0053__thermal-coal` at
**rank 107**. The gold chunk was beaten by 106 chunks the dense
embedding considered more semantically similar — including Swiss
Re's parallel thermal-coal chunk at rank 1.

A **self-retrieval sanity check** confirmed the chunk's vector is
valid: querying with the chunk's own first 200 characters retrieved
it at rank 1 with score 0.811, with a substantial gap to rank 2
(0.696). The index is sound; the asymmetry is between query and
chunk *representations*.

**Alternative-phrasing probe** then localised the asymmetry
precisely:

| Phrasing | Gold rank |
|---|---|
| "thermal coal mines coal-fired power plants insurance" | 1 |
| "Munich Re no longer insures thermal coal mines" | 3 |
| "Munich Re thermal coal" | not in top-5 |
| "stand-alone risks thermal coal Munich Re" | not in top-5 |

The pattern is that queries phrased like the chunk's **claim verbs**
("no longer insures") embed close to it. Queries phrased like
**meta-policy questions** ("what is X's policy on Y") embed close
to other chunks that themselves discuss policies meta-statically —
investment policy chunks, decarbonisation approach chunks, etc.

### Diagnostic conclusion: query/chunk language asymmetry

The Q12 root cause is a well-understood limitation of single-vector
dense retrieval. CLS-pooled BGE-M3 (per D010) encodes *what this
passage is about* at a topical level. The gold chunk is about a
specific operational policy; the benchmark query is phrased as a
meta-statement about a policy class. These land in different
semantic neighbourhoods of the embedding space.

RRF fusion cannot rescue chunks the dense channel ranks at #107
because the candidate set realistically caps below 500. The
asymmetry is structural, not a tuning issue.

### Remediation deferred (Q13)

Three remediation paths were scoped, each real engineering work
outside the scope of this initial build:

1. **LLM query rewriting / HyDE.** Use the LLM to expand or rephrase
   the query before embedding. Estimated 1–2 hours of work plus
   per-query LLM latency.
2. **Cross-encoder reranker.** Fetch a wide candidate set, then
   re-rank the top-50 with a cross-encoder. ~3–4 hours plus
   reranking latency.
3. **BGE-M3 multi-vector channel (Q7 revisit).** Move from
   single-vector MLX to FlagEmbedding for full multi-functionality
   including ColBERT-style late interaction. ~4–8 hours, breaks
   the MLX-everywhere posture of D009.

Q13 records the three paths as the v2 work-stream. None was
implemented in v1 because the available data already cleanly
falsified two cheaper hypotheses (top_k, RRF tuning), and a
half-implemented reranker would have introduced more uncertainty
than it removed.

### Meta-finding worth recording

The Q12 arc is the project's clearest example of "form hypothesis,
design test that can falsify it, accept the falsification." Probes
1 and 2 each took under a minute to design and execute; either
could have surfaced before any code change. The wrong discipline —
"form hypothesis, implement the fix without testing it, ship and
hope" — would have produced a half-tuned reranker that did not
address the underlying asymmetry. The right discipline produced a
diagnosis cheap enough to fit in the budget alongside the rest of
Day 4.

---

## The N=70 Extension {#sec-exp-n70}

After Q12 closed, two questions remained open:

- **Q11 — production model choice.** The N=40 data supported a Gemma
  default on hallucination floor and on cross-document synthesis,
  but the latter was N=2 — too small to drive a production decision
  on its own.
- **Within-document parity claim.** The N=40 "both models 0.929 on
  within-document-retrievable" finding looked clean but was
  potentially partition-sensitive.

To pressure-test both, the benchmark was extended from 40 to 70
questions on Day-5 morning, by adding more questions in each
existing category. The extended sweep ran the same 2×2 grid as the
original, producing 280 cells (vs the original 160). Wall-clock
extended to 45.7 minutes; zero errored cells.

The N=70 benchmark composition:

| Partition | N=40 | N=70 |
|---|---|---|
| Answerable | 26 | 44 |
| Refusal | 14 | 26 |
| Cross-document subset | 2 | 10 |

The cross-document subset expansion from N=2 to N=10 was the most
load-bearing single change, because it is the subset that drives
the production model choice.

---

## N=70 Results: Two Updates to the Record {#sec-exp-n70-results}

The full per-cell headline from the N=70 extended sweep:

| Cell | citation_recall | citation_precision | hallucinations | refusals | latency (ans / ref) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen × v1 | 0.386 | 0.296 | 23 | 26/26 | 3.3s / 0.8s |
| Qwen × v2 | 0.545 | 0.483 | 7 | 26/26 | 3.4s / 0.9s |
| Gemma × v1 | 0.598 | 0.524 | 0 | 26/26 | 21.7s / 8.4s |
| **Gemma × v2** | **0.598** | **0.520** | **0** | **26/26** | **22.9s / 8.6s** |

Two updates to the record follow from these numbers.

### Update 1 — Family-axis retraction holds (and strengthens)

On the extended sample, the **Qwen v2 — Gemma v2 gap is 5.3
percentage points** (0.545 vs 0.598), still well inside the
10-point threshold. The Qwen prompt-v2 movement is +15.9 percentage
points (0.386 → 0.545), against Gemma's prompt-robust 0.598 on
both prompts. The v1 hallucination count on Qwen also drops sharply
under v2 (23 → 7). The Day-3 retraction of the family-axis
interpretation is reaffirmed by the larger sample.

The gap widens slightly relative to N=40 (5.3pp at N=70 vs 3.2pp
at N=40), consistent with the *within-document parity weakening*
recorded as Update 2 below — the additional questions at N=70
exercise more challenging within-document subsets where Gemma's
small edge becomes visible.

### Update 2 — Within-document parity weakens

The N=40 "0.929 / 0.929 within-document parity" claim does **not**
survive the larger sample. Subset analysis from the N=70 report:

| Subset | n | Qwen × v2 | Gemma × v2 | gap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `single_chunk_retrievable` | 17 | 0.941 | 1.000 | 5.9pp |
| `within_document_retrievable` | 27 | 0.833 | 0.889 | 5.6pp |
| `multi_chunk` | 12 | 0.542 | 0.583 | 4.1pp |

Gemma now leads on every within-document subset. The gap is small
(~5pp), consistent across subsets, and not concentrated in any one
question type — which is what a real but partition-sensitive
advantage looks like, distinct from a sample-noise artefact (which
would scatter directionally) or a load-bearing model property
(which would manifest as a wider, more chapter-skewed gap).

The Day-4 within-document parity claim is recorded as **weakened,
not retracted**. The qualitative point — that prompt v2 closes
most of the gap on within-document workloads — survives. The
quantitative point — that the gap closes to zero — does not.

This is the project's second retraction on the record. The journal
entry that records it is honest about what survives and what does
not, and the section's headline numbers throughout this report are
the N=70 figures, not the N=40 figures.

### Update 3 — Cross-document is hard for everything tested

The cross-document subset expansion to N=10 makes the cross-document
finding load-bearing rather than indicative:

| Subset | n | Qwen × v1 | Qwen × v2 | Gemma × v1 | Gemma × v2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `cross_document` | 10 | 0.150 | 0.150 | 0.233 | 0.233 |

All four cells score below 0.25 mean citation_recall. Gemma
maintains a small lead but no cell is close to acceptable. The
production system **must not be relied on for cross-document
synthesis as a primary use case**, and the governance document
commits to this explicitly. Whether the gap is a model-capability
limit, a retrieval limit (cross-document questions exercise the
same retrieval pipeline that has a 25% miss rate; the multi-issuer
retrieval problem is harder than the single-issuer one), or both,
is not resolved by these data and is recorded as future work.

### Update 4 — Retrieval miss rate worsens at scale

The N=40 retrieval miss rate was 11.5% (3/26). On N=70 it is
**25.0% (11/44)**. The Q12 diagnostic is the same — query/chunk
language asymmetry on single-vector dense pooling — and the
remediation menu (Q13) is unchanged. But the size of the underlying
problem is roughly twice what N=40 suggested, which firms up the
priority of Q13 in v2 planning.

---

## Q11 Resolved: D015 {#sec-exp-q11-resolution}

With Q12 closed (diagnosed, deferred) and the N=70 data in hand,
the production model decision Q11 resolved cleanly:

- **Citation discipline:** Gemma's hallucination floor at 0 (vs
  Qwen v2 at 7) and Gemma's robustness to prompt change (recall
  identical under v1 and v2) both argue for Gemma.
- **Within-document quality:** Gemma leads by ~5pp on the N=70
  subsets. Small but consistent.
- **Cross-document quality:** Gemma leads on N=10 (0.233 vs
  0.150), but neither cell is good enough to make the difference
  decisive.
- **Refusal correctness:** tied (both models 100%).
- **Latency:** Qwen wins ~6×. Acceptable cost for an underwriting-
  research workflow where the alternative is a human spending
  several minutes on the same query.

**D015 was lodged with Gemma 4 31B IT as the production default.**
The decision includes the `UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` env-var
override path, so deployments with stricter latency budgets can
swap to Qwen without code change. A status note appended to D015
on 2026-06-18 records that the N=70 sweep strengthened the
rationale: the within-document advantage was clearer at the larger
sample than the N=40 parity finding had suggested.

---

## What Was Deliberately Not Tested {#sec-exp-not-tested}

Three potential evaluation extensions were considered and **not**
run in v1, with the deferral recorded so a reviewer can see the
boundary of the evidence base.

- **Multi-seed run-variance.** Each cell of the sweep is a single
  run. The deterministic temperature-0 setting and pinned RNG seeds
  make the cell results bit-reproducible *for the same software
  state*, but the eval does not measure run-to-run variance from
  re-seeded sweeps. The qualitative findings (the retraction, the
  weakening, the diagnostic) are robust to any plausible
  single-seed variance, but precise digits past the first decimal
  should be read with that caveat.

- **LLM-as-judge semantic correctness.** The scorer measures whether
  the model cited the chunks we expected it to cite; it does not
  measure whether the model's natural-language prose *accurately
  reflects* what those chunks say. An LLM-as-judge layer (e.g.
  Claude scoring whether the answer is supported by the cited
  chunks) is recorded as Q10.3 and deferred. Until that layer
  lands, "citation correctness" should be read as a structural
  proxy for semantic correctness, not as semantic correctness
  itself.

- **A wider model survey.** The 2×2 grid tested two specific
  candidates because they were the ones that ran cleanly on the
  oMLX stack at a usable size. Gemma 4 12B was attempted and
  blocked at the mlx_vlm layer (Q9, closed-deferred). A broader
  survey — Llama 3 70B variants, smaller Gemma sizes, the Qwen
  reasoning variants — would strengthen generalisation but does
  not fit within scope.

- **GEPA / DSPy systematic prompt optimisation (Q10).** After D014's
  hand-designed v2 closed most of the family-axis gap, the case
  for layering DSPy's GEPA optimiser on top weakened to
  "exploratory, not load-bearing." Q10 remains open as a possible
  v2 work-stream.

---

## Experiments at a Glance {#sec-exp-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Sweep design** | 2×2 grid: models × prompts, fully crossed; same questions in every cell |
| **Models swept** | `gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit`, `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit` (`enable_thinking=False`) |
| **Prompts swept** | v1 (literal `[chunk_id]` placeholder), v2 (`<ID>` metasyntax + worked example) |
| **Benchmark progression** | N=40 (canonical D014) → N=70 (extension); both runs committed |
| **N=40 wall-clock** | 23.7 min, 160 cells, 0 errored |
| **N=70 wall-clock** | 45.7 min, 280 cells, 0 errored |
| **Pre-stated falsification criterion** | Qwen v2 closes gap to Gemma to ≤10pp on citation_recall + Qwen v2 hallucinations ≤ 2× Gemma's |
| **Observed (N=40)** | Qwen v2 − Gemma v2 gap = 3.2pp → recall criterion met decisively; hallucination criterion fails on strict read (2 × 0 = 0) but Qwen v1 → v2 hallucinations 15 → 3 → **family-axis retracted** |
| **Observed (N=70)** | Qwen v2 − Gemma v2 gap = 5.3pp → recall criterion still met; retraction reaffirmed |
| **Retractions on record** | 2 — family-axis (N=40) and within-document parity (N=70 weakening) |
| **Q12 outcome** | Closed-diagnosed; root cause = query/chunk language asymmetry on CLS-pooled dense embeddings |
| **Q11 outcome** | Resolved → D015 (Gemma 4 31B IT as production default) |
| **Q13 status** | Open v2 work-stream: HyDE-style query expansion / cross-encoder reranker / BGE-M3 multi-vector revisit |
| **Reproduction** | `uv run python -m eval.runner` (full sweep, ~46 min) + `uv run python -m eval.report` (regenerate `report.md` from `raw.jsonl`) |
| **Canonical runs** | `eval/results/2026-06-18T12-16-35Z/` (N=40) · `eval/results/2026-06-18T15-32-07Z/` (N=70) |
